data.title: Titan Acquisition announces pricing of $240M IPO
data.description:
• Titan Acquisition (TACHU) Tuesday announced the pricing of its initial public offering of 24,000,000 units at $10.00 per unit.
  • The units are expected to be listed on the Nasdaq Global Market and trade under the ticker symbol “TACHU” beginning April 9, 2025.
  • Each unit consists of one Class A ordinary share and one-half of one redeemable warrant.
  • Once the securities comprising the units begin separate trading, the Class A ordinary shares and warrants are expected to be listed on Nasdaq under the symbols “TACH” and “TACHW”, respectively.

data.title: Aris Mining Q1 total gold production up 8%
data.description:
• Aris Mining (NYSE:ARMN) Tuesday reported gold production of 54,763 ounces ((oz)) for the first quarter of 2025, up 8% from a year ago.
  • Q1 2025 production reflects strong operational momentum heading into 2025.
  • With the commissioning of the expanded Segovia processing facility on track for Q2 2025, additional production growth is anticipated in the coming quarters.
  • Aris Mining's 2025 full-year production guidance is between 230,000 to 275,000 ((oz)), with Segovia expected to produce 210,000 to 250,000 ((oz)) and the Marmato Upper Mine expected to contribute 20,000 to 25,000 ((oz)).

data.title: Asia-Pacific markets react negatively as Trump's country-specific tariffs begin today
data.description:
Asian stocks mostly decline on Wednesday, taking cues from a negative lead on Wall Street as US President Donald Trump’s new tariffs are set to come into effect later today, also mounting concerns that President Donald Trump’s tariffs could push the economy into a recession and force the Federal Reserve to cut rates further.

Trump’s reciprocal tariffs came into effect today, including a cumulative 104% levy on Chinese imports.

Traders were also disappointed by the lack of concrete developments in trade negotiations, even though President Trump spoke with various leaders from major trading partners.

According to the Asian Development Outlook, developing Asian economies are projected to see a gradual deceleration in GDP growth, from 5.0% in 2024 to 4.9% in 2025 (the slowest since 2022) and further to 4.7% in 2026.

Meanwhile, investors are awaiting the release of the U.S. Federal Reserve’s March policy meeting minutes, due later in the day, to gauge policymakers' rate cut pain point.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 4.19% toward 31,800 while the broader Topix Index tumbled 2.9% to 2,362 on Wednesday, giving back gains from the previous session. The Japanese yen appreciated past 146 per dollar on Wednesday, rising for the second straight session.

Japan also faces a 24% tariff on its goods and a 25% levy on car shipments to the US. Meanwhile, Trump said that Japan is sending a team to negotiate on trade with the US, while Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba revealed that he told Trump in a phone call to rethink his tariff policies.

On the domestic front, Japan’s current account surplus surged to a record high in February, driven by strong export growth and a decline in imports, adding further support to the yen.

The consumer confidence index in Japan dropped to 34.1 in March 2025 from a downwardly revised 34.8 in the previous month, declining for the fourth straight month and reaching its lowest level since March 2023.

China (SHCOMP) rose 0.21% to below 3,100 while the Shenzhen Component lost 0.9% to 9,340 on Wednesday, reversing gains from the previous session as US President Donald Trump is set to impose a hefty 104% tariff on China in what appears to be an all-out trade war, and the offshore yuan climbed toward 7.38 per dollar on Wednesday, rebounding from three consecutive sessions of losses, supported by a depreciating US dollar. 

In the meantime, Beijing on Tuesday vowed to “fight to the end” to protect its interests, accusing the U.S. of engaging in economic blackmail, contrasts with the White House's claim that Trump won't back down on tariffs, and that China wants a deal, but doesn't know how to make one.

Meanwhile, during a call with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen on Tuesday, Premier Li Qiang emphasized that China’s macroeconomic policies are designed to address uncertainties and expressed confidence in sustained growth this year. Both China and Europe are preparing for significant impacts from new U.S. tariffs, with China facing a 104% surtax and Europe a 20% levy.

Caution also grew ahead of China’s March CPI and PPI data due later this week, with deflation risks still looming. 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 1.78% to 19,662 in early trade on Wednesday, reversing strong gains from the previous session. 

India (SENSEX) fell 0.53% to 73,865 in early deals on Wednesday, erasing gains from the previous session.

However, the RBI's decision to cut interest rates by 25 bps to 6% capped the fall, despite the central bank revising its GDP forecast downward to 6.5% from 6.7% for FY2025-26, while inflation was revised to 4% from 4.2%.

Australia (AS51) fell 1.91% to around 7,360 on Wednesday, reversing gains from the previous session. The Australian dollar appreciated toward $0.60 on Wednesday, rebounding slightly from five-year lows.

Australia will face the minimum 10% levy, with Trump taking a particular interest in blocking Australian beef amid long-standing restrictions on US beef exports to the country.

In the U.S., on Tuesday, all three major indexes ended in red as optimism over global tariff negotiations soured after President Trump confirmed the imposition of a 104% tariff on China, effective tomorrow.

Meanwhile, the Treasury Department auctioned $58 billion in 3-year notes on Tuesday, marking the first coupon supply since the tariff announcement.

U.S. stock futures declined Wednesday as President Donald Trump’s sweeping tariffs came into effect today, including a 104% levy on Chinese imports: Dow -1.88%; S&P 500 -2.04%; Nasdaq -2.08%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia:

  • China pledges to 'fight to the end' against new U.S. tariffs, escalating the trade dispute

  • China's trade war escalation is 'big mistake,' Treasury's Bessent says

  • Asia stocks under pressure; China, Japan see significant losses, down about 7% amid trade war

  • Asia panic selling: Circuit breakers triggered in Japan, South Korea and Taiwan

  • China, EU revive EV tariff talks following U.S. trade actions

data.title: Ex-Meta employee to tell Congress that company helped China in AI race, undermined U.S. security
Trump says 'major tariff on pharmaceuticals' coming soon
data.description:
• President Donald Trump on Tuesday said that the U.S. will soon introduce a "major" tariff on pharmaceutical imports.
  • “We’re going to tariff our pharmaceuticals, and once we do that, they are going to come rushing back into our country, because we’re the big market," Trump said during remarks at a dinner in Washington, D.C.
  • The President earlier this month said that several large drugmakers -- Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Merck (MRK) -- have made announcements of making manufacturing investments in the U.S.
  • VanEck Vectors Pharmaceutical ETF (PPH), which represents 25 of the leading global drugmakers, has slipped 6.4% year-to-date, with the S&P 500 Health Care Index Sector (NYSEARCA:XLV) down 3.3%.

data.title: UniFirst declares $0.35 dividend, $100M buyback
data.description:
• UniFirst (NYSE:UNF) declares $0.35/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 0.86%
  • Payable June 27; for shareholders of record June 6; ex-div June 6.
  • Board of Directors authorized a new share repurchase program allowing the company to repurchase up to $100.0 million of its outstanding common shares, inclusive of the amount which remained available under the existing share repurchase program approved in October 2023.
  • See UNF Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Wednesday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
07:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications

The data point measures the change in the number of new applications for mortgages backed by the MBA during the reported week.

10:00 AM Wholesale Inventories (Preliminary)

Wholesale trade measures the dollar value of sales made and inventories held by merchant wholesalers. It is a component of business sales and inventories. Forecasters see no revision from the flash at 0.3 percent on month for February.

10:30 AM EIA Petroleum Status Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on petroleum inventories in the U.S., whether produced here or abroad. The level of inventories helps determine prices for petroleum products.

11:00 AM Treasury Buyback Announcement

The Treasury conducts buybacks to improve liquidity for certain maturities, limit variations in auction sizes, and reduce maturity peaks in outstanding debt.

12:30 PM Fed's Barkin Speech

Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin will participate in a conversation with the Economic Club of Washington.

1:00 PM 10-Yr Note Auction

The US Treasury Department will hold an auction for $39 billion of 10-year Notes.

2:00 PM FOMC Minutes

FOMC discloses policy changes at the end of each meeting. The Fed's minutes are a market mover as investors and analysts parse each word looking for clues to policy.

data.title: Kura Sushi outlines fiscal 2025 growth plans with targeted 20% unit expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kura Sushi USA, Inc. (KRUS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Hajime Uba highlighted the company’s progress in expanding into new markets, including Bakersfield, California, which has performed strongly despite being a smaller DMA. He announced the opening of 11 new restaurants year-to-date and six more under construction, keeping the company on track for its target of 14 new units in fiscal 2025.
  • Total sales for the quarter were $64.9 million, with comparable sales declining 5.3%. Factors impacting performance included weather-related disruptions and a lack of an IP collaboration compared to the same period last year.
  • CEO Uba emphasized the significance of new initiatives, such as the reservation system and redesigned Mr. Fresh dome, which aim to improve operational efficiency and enhance guest experience.
  • CFO Jeff Uttz reported restaurant-level operating profit margins of 17.3%, down from 19.6% a year ago, citing sales deleverage as a key factor. He reiterated the company's fiscal 2025 sales guidance of $275 million to $279 million and maintained the planned 20% unit growth rate.

Outlook

  • The company expects the roll-out of its reservation system across all locations by the end of fiscal 2025, aiming to drive traffic and improve guest satisfaction. It also plans to execute seven or eight IP collaborations in fiscal 2026, ensuring no gaps between campaigns.
  • Labor cost management remains a focus, with CEO Uba noting that while high labor market inflation has been a challenge, new technology initiatives are expected to alleviate pressures.
  • Management reaffirmed its fiscal 2025 guidance, including sales between $275 million and $279 million and general and administrative expenses at approximately 13.5% of total sales.

Financial Results

  • Total sales for Q2 2025 were $64.9 million, compared to $57.3 million in the prior-year period. Comparable sales growth was impacted by a 5.3% decline, driven by an 8.5% drop in traffic, partially offset by a 3.2% price and mix increase.
  • Food and beverage costs improved to 28.7% of sales, down from 29.6% in the prior year, due to supply chain initiatives and pricing.
  • Labor costs increased to 34.8% of sales from 33%, reflecting wage inflation and sales deleverage.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $2.7 million, slightly down from $2.9 million in the prior year.
  • Net loss widened to $3.8 million or ($0.31) per share, compared to a net loss of $1 million or ($0.09) per share in Q2 2024.

Q&A

  • Andrew Charles, TD Cowen: Asked about the impact of inclement weather on trends and whether performance improved post-January and February. CEO Uba responded that March saw smooth performance but noted uncertainties related to recent tariff announcements.
  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: Inquired about the potential consumer impact of tariffs and the company’s supply chain resilience. CFO Uttz stated that suppliers had shown willingness to share tariff impacts, and the company’s strong cash position provides flexibility.
  • Jeremy Hamblin, Craig-Hallum Capital Group: Asked about the cadence of restaurant openings. CEO Uba confirmed one additional opening for Q3 and highlighted challenges with certain construction timelines.
  • JP Wollam, ROTH Capital Partners: Queried the timeline for the reservation system rollout. Management noted the system is expected to be operational across all locations by fiscal year-end.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on consumer spending trends and the company’s ability to manage costs amid inflation and tariffs.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing new initiatives and operational efficiencies during prepared remarks. However, in responding to analysts, there was some acknowledgment of uncertainties, particularly around tariffs and consumer behavior.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared more focused on external pressures, while management reiterated its long-term confidence in strategic initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Key financial metrics, such as restaurant-level operating profit margins, declined from the prior quarter due to sales deleverage and higher labor costs.
  • Strategic focus shifted from IP collaborations in Q1 to operational improvements and marketing adjustments in Q2. Management highlighted the continued strong performance of new units, particularly in smaller markets like Bakersfield.
  • Analysts’ tone shifted slightly towards concerns about external factors, such as tariffs and consumer spending.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the potential impact of tariffs on supply chain costs but emphasized its ability to mitigate these through supplier negotiations.
  • Analysts raised concerns about consumer spending trends and the potential for further sales deleverage in the face of economic uncertainty.
  • Labor cost pressures remain a challenge, although management is optimistic about technological mitigations.

Final Takeaway

Kura Sushi USA continues to focus on expanding its market presence and enhancing operational efficiency through technological innovations like a new reservation system and streamlined in-store processes. While sales were impacted by weather disruptions and the absence of IP collaborations, the company remains confident in achieving its fiscal 2025 targets. Analysts are closely monitoring external factors, including tariffs and consumer behavior, which could influence the company’s near-term performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Peabody Energy surges on Trump's executive orders boosting coal industry
data.description:
Peabody Energy (NYSE:BTU) +15.3% post-market Tuesday, adding to a 9.2% gain in the regular session, as President Trump signed executive orders intended to expand the production and use of coal in the U.S. after years of declines.

Trump said he would use the Defense Production Act to boost coal mining, a move that could put the federal purse behind reviving the industry.

The president cast the actions as essential to helping the U.S. dominate the rapidly developing artificial intelligence sector, saying the country needed to ramp up electricity production to assure its position in the AI race.

Trump said he would direct Energy Secretary Chris Wright to invest in next-generation coal technology and ask the Department of Justice to identify and fight state and local regulations he said were putting coal miners out of work.

Other coal stocks also posted solid gains during the regular session: Ramaco Resources (METC) +13.9%, Core Natural Resources (CNR) +6.6%, Warrior Met Coal (HCC) +6.3%, Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP) +4.8%, Alpha Metallurgical Resources (AMR) +2.4%, SunCoke Energy (SXC) +1.2%.
